text: Let's open up the engine.
Nexus is a contextual, multi-armed bandit.
Every hour, it chooses the best message for each user,
learns from what happens, and gets a little sharper.
Here are the ten steps, end to end.
Step one: data sync.
It all begins with behavior.
On a schedule, Hightouch syncs your CRM into Nexus.
Each user's channel subscriptions, last-seen time,
funnel stage, and engagement history
land in a single TrackedUser record.
That record is the raw material for everything that follows.
Step two: the feature vector.
Raw history is messy, so Nexus distills it.
Ten behavioral signals per user, each a single number.
Push open rate, email click rate,
morning, evening, and weekend ratios,
conversion rate, recency,
giving tier, spiritual depth, and engagement frequency.
Ten dimensions that capture how someone behaves.
Step three: persona assignment.
Now Nexus looks for structure.
K-means clustering groups similar vectors together.
caption: k-means clustering groups similar vectors together.

text: Four to nine behavioral archetypes emerge, called personas.
Each user is matched to their nearest cluster.
No one hand-builds these segments.
They're discovered from behavior, and re-clustered as it shifts.
Step four: agents and variants.
An agent defines the what and the who.
Its message variants are the bandit's arms,
each one a candidate message.
It targets specific personas,
sets frequency caps and quiet hours, and an audience size.
This is the only part a human configures.
From here, Nexus takes over.
Step five: the hourly eligibility check.
Every hour, a cron scans the active agents.
For each, it filters to who is eligible right now.
Is the channel subscribed?
Is the frequency cap still open?
Are we outside quiet hours, and inside the audience cap?
Only the eligible users move forward.
Step six: arm selection — the heart of it.
For each eligible user, Nexus looks up their persona.
Within that persona, every arm has a Beta distribution
of successes and failures.
Nexus draws a random sample from each arm's Beta,
and sends the variant with the highest draw.
Strong arms get sampled higher over time — that's exploitation.
Uncertain arms still get their shot — that's exploration.
Step seven: the send.
The winning variant is dispatched to Braze,
as a push, an email, or an in-app message.
And the decision is logged in full —
the user, the variant, the channel, the scheduled time —
ready to be matched against what happens next.
Step eight: observing engagement.
Now Nexus waits, and watches.
When the user opens, taps, or converts,
that event flows back through Hightouch, into the ingest endpoint.
It's matched to the logged decision
by user id, within a twenty four hour window.
Action, tied back to the message that caused it.
Step nine: the reward.
A matched event becomes a number.
It's tiered by goal —
a reading-plan completion outweighs a simple open.
It's normalized, and time-discounted
by how quickly the user engaged.
A positive reward raises alpha on the winning arm.
No conversion raises beta.
The arm's belief gets one step sharper.
Step ten: the loop tightens.
Updated distributions mean better samples next round.
The strong arms pull ahead; the weak ones fade.
Over thousands of sends,
each persona's picture comes into focus.
More personalized. More precise.
Better with every single cycle.
Ten steps, every hour, on a loop.
Raw behavior in. Sharper decisions out.
Nexus. A contextual bandit for lifecycle messaging.
